# OAuth-Conformant `github-app-token-broker` On Cloud Run

## Summary

Build `github-app-token-broker` as an OAuth 2.0 Token Exchange authorization server deployed on Cloud Run. The endpoint remains `POST /token`, accepts `application/x-www-form-urlencoded`, and always identifies the requesting principal through RFC 8693 `subject_token`.

Yes: the Google service account OIDC token should be supplied in `subject_token`, not primarily in `Authorization`. RFC 8693 makes `subject_token` required and defines it as the token representing the party on behalf of whom the request is made. `Authorization` is OAuth client authentication, not the exchanged subject token.

Use GCP native capabilities around the OAuth endpoint rather than changing the OAuth wire shape: Cloud Run for compute, Secret Manager for GitHub App private keys, Artifact Registry and Cloud Build for delivery, Cloud Logging and Monitoring for operations, and Cloud Armor for public-edge protection. Do not require Cloud Run IAM invocation on the canonical endpoint because Cloud Run IAM authenticates header tokens, while the OAuth-conformant token exchange subject is in the form body.

Standards and platform references:
- RFC 8693 request parameters: https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8693.html#section-2.1
- RFC 8693 token type identifiers: https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8693.html#section-3
- OAuth 2.0 token endpoint/client auth: https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc6749.html
- OpenID Connect ID Token claims: https://openid.net/specs/openid-connect-core-1_0.html#IDToken
- Google ID tokens: https://docs.cloud.google.com/docs/authentication/get-id-token
- Google token types/service account ID tokens: https://docs.cloud.google.com/docs/authentication/token-types
- Cloud Run service-to-service auth headers: https://docs.cloud.google.com/run/docs/authenticating/service-to-service
- Cloud Run secrets: https://docs.cloud.google.com/run/docs/configuring/services/secrets

## Public OAuth Contract

- Request:
  - `POST /token`
  - `Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded`
  - `grant_type=urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:token-exchange`
  - `subject_token=<OIDC ID token>`
  - `subject_token_type=urn:ietf:params:oauth:token-type:id_token`
  - `requested_token_type=urn:chikachow:github-app-installation-access-token`
  - `audience=https://github.com/apps/{github-app-slug}`
  - optional `resource=https://api.github.com/repos/{owner}/{repo}`
  - optional `scope=<github-app-permission-scope>`
- `subject_token` may be either:
  - GitHub Actions OIDC ID token from `https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com`
  - Google service account ID token from `https://accounts.google.com`
- `Authorization` header is rejected in v1 with `401 invalid_client`; the broker does not support OAuth client authentication yet.
- `actor_token`, `actor_token_type`, `client_id`, `client_secret`, `client_assertion`, `client_assertion_type`, and `authorization_details` remain unsupported and rejected.
- Response stays RFC 8693/OAuth-shaped:
  - `access_token`
  - `issued_token_type`
  - `token_type=Bearer`
  - `scope`
  - `expires_in`
  - `Cache-Control: no-store`
  - `Pragma: no-cache`

## Authentication And Policy

- Add issuer-aware subject-token verification:
  - GitHub Actions verifier checks issuer, JWKS, `RS256`, expiry, required claims, exact `audience` match, and `azp` if present.
  - Google service account verifier checks issuer `https://accounts.google.com`, Google JWKS, expiry, `subject_token_type=id_token`, `aud` equals the broker’s configured subject-token audience, `email`, `email_verified=true`, and stable numeric `sub`.
- Use a single issuance policy over one principal and one requested GitHub App token:
  ```ts
  {
    principal:
      | { kind: "github_actions"; repository; ref; workflowRef; eventName; subject; ... }
      | { kind: "google_service_account"; email; subject; audience; ... },
    tokenRequest: { githubAppSlug; resource; permissions; scope }
  }
  ```
- Policy rules are exact-match and deny-by-default:
  ```json
  {
    "id": "gcp-deploy-service-can-write-target-repo",
    "principal": {
      "kind": "google_service_account",
      "email": "deploy-service@PROJECT.iam.gserviceaccount.com",
      "subject": "112010400000000710080"
    },
    "tokenRequest": {
      "githubAppSlug": "example-app",
      "resource": "https://api.github.com/repos/OWNER/REPO",
      "permissions": { "contents": "write" }
    }
  }
  ```
- Keep checked-in policy for v1. Do not move policy to Secret Manager; it is authorization data, not secret material.
- Log structured deny reasons without logging raw OIDC tokens or issued GitHub tokens.

## GCP Platform Integration

- Cloud Run:
  - Deploy one public service: `github-app-token-broker`.
  - Public invocation is intentional; app-level OAuth/OIDC verification is authoritative.
  - Runtime identity: `github-app-token-broker-runtime`.
  - Configure env vars:
    - `BROKER_SUBJECT_TOKEN_AUDIENCE=https://github-app-token-broker.<domain>`
    - `GITHUB_API_BASE_URL=https://api.github.com`
    - GitHub App ID mapping by slug.
- GCP callers:
  - Obtain a Google-signed service account ID token using metadata server, Google auth libraries, IAM Credentials API, Workflows, or equivalent.
  - Set its `aud` to `BROKER_SUBJECT_TOKEN_AUDIENCE`.
  - Send that ID token as `subject_token` in the OAuth token exchange body.
  - Do not rely on Cloud Run’s `Authorization` or `X-Serverless-Authorization` header path for the canonical OAuth exchange.
- Edge and operations:
  - Put Cloud Run behind HTTPS Load Balancer + serverless NEG.
  - Use Cloud Armor for rate limiting and coarse request protection.
  - Keep app-level `64 KiB` body limit.
  - Use Cloud Logging, Monitoring, Error Reporting, and alerting on 5xx, policy-deny spikes, OIDC provider failures, GitHub API failures, and Cloud Armor rate-limit events.
- Secret Manager:
  - Store each GitHub App private key as a separate secret, e.g. `github-app-token-broker-github-app-<slug>-private-key`.
  - Prefer mounted secret volumes for rotation-sensitive keys; Cloud Run fetches mounted secrets from Secret Manager at read time.
  - If using env vars, pin explicit secret versions because Cloud Run resolves secret env vars at instance startup.
  - Grant `roles/secretmanager.secretAccessor` only on required secrets to the runtime service account.
  - Rotate by adding a new secret version, deploying or refreshing the Cloud Run revision, validating issuance, then disabling the old version.
- Delivery:
  - Build with Cloud Build.
  - Store images in Artifact Registry.
  - Deploy with a dedicated deploy service account.
  - Use Workload Identity Federation for CI/CD; no service account keys.

## Implementation Changes

- Rename all deployable service/package/docs/logging identifiers away from `cyspbot`; use `github-app-token-broker`.
- Preserve the existing parser discipline:
  - exactly one non-empty value for required consumed parameters
  - duplicate consumed parameters rejected
  - unsupported extension parameters rejected when non-empty
  - optional empty `scope` and `resource` treated as omitted
- Refactor authentication into:
  - `SubjectTokenVerifier`
  - `GitHubActionsSubjectTokenVerifier`
  - `GoogleServiceAccountSubjectTokenVerifier`
  - `IssuancePrincipal`
- Refactor token policy from GitHub-only principal matching to issuer-aware principal matching.
- Keep token request normalization shared across issuers:
  - GitHub App slug from `audience`
  - canonical GitHub API repo URI from `resource`
  - permissions from OAuth `scope`
- For Google service account subjects, omitted `resource` must be rejected with `invalid_target`; unlike GitHub Actions, there is no source repository claim to default from.
- Keep GitHub Actions default `resource` behavior from the verified GitHub principal repository.

## Test Plan

- OAuth conformance:
  - missing `subject_token` rejected
  - missing `subject_token_type` rejected
  - unsupported `subject_token_type` rejected
  - `Authorization` rejected as unsupported client auth
  - `actor_token` and malformed actor fields rejected
  - success response includes required RFC 8693 fields
- Google service account principal:
  - valid Google ID token in `subject_token` allows matching policy
  - wrong issuer rejected
  - wrong `aud` rejected
  - missing `email`, `email_verified=false`, missing `sub`, expired token, and unknown signing key rejected
  - omitted `resource` rejected for Google principals
- GitHub Actions principal:
  - existing allow/deny tests preserved
  - exact audience match preserved
  - `azp` mismatch rejected
  - omitted `resource` still defaults to source repository
- Policy:
  - GitHub rule cannot match Google principal
  - Google rule cannot match GitHub principal
  - wrong service account email or subject denied
  - wrong GitHub App, resource, or permissions denied with precise reasons
- GCP integration:
  - Cloud Run caller obtains ID token for `BROKER_SUBJECT_TOKEN_AUDIENCE` and sends it as `subject_token`
  - Secret Manager key access works with runtime service account
  - Cloud Armor rate limit triggers before high-volume traffic reaches the app
  - logs contain principal kind, principal identity, policy decision, GitHub App slug, resource, and permissions, but no raw tokens

## Assumptions And Defaults

- Canonical OAuth endpoint is public at the Cloud Run edge and authenticated in application code.
- `subject_token` is the only request principal source.
- Google service account callers use ID tokens, not access tokens.

<proposed_plan>
# OAuth-Conformant `github-app-token-broker` On Cloud Run

## Summary

Build `github-app-token-broker` as an OAuth 2.0 Token Exchange authorization server deployed on Cloud Run. The endpoint remains `POST /token`, accepts `application/x-www-form-urlencoded`, and always identifies the requesting principal through RFC 8693 `subject_token`.

Yes: the Google service account OIDC token should be supplied in `subject_token`, not primarily in `Authorization`. RFC 8693 makes `subject_token` required and defines it as the token representing the party on behalf of whom the request is made. `Authorization` is OAuth client authentication, not the exchanged subject token.

Use GCP native capabilities around the OAuth endpoint rather than changing the OAuth wire shape: Cloud Run for compute, Secret Manager for GitHub App private keys, Artifact Registry and Cloud Build for delivery, Cloud Logging and Monitoring for operations, and Cloud Armor for public-edge protection. Do not require Cloud Run IAM invocation on the canonical endpoint because Cloud Run IAM authenticates header tokens, while the OAuth-conformant token exchange subject is in the form body.

Standards and platform references:
- RFC 8693 request parameters: https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8693.html#section-2.1
- RFC 8693 token type identifiers: https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8693.html#section-3
- OAuth 2.0 token endpoint/client auth: https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc6749.html
- OpenID Connect ID Token claims: https://openid.net/specs/openid-connect-core-1_0.html#IDToken
- Google ID tokens: https://docs.cloud.google.com/docs/authentication/get-id-token
- Google token types/service account ID tokens: https://docs.cloud.google.com/docs/authentication/token-types
- Cloud Run service-to-service auth headers: https://docs.cloud.google.com/run/docs/authenticating/service-to-service
- Cloud Run secrets: https://docs.cloud.google.com/run/docs/configuring/services/secrets

## Public OAuth Contract

- Request:
  - `POST /token`
  - `Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded`
  - `grant_type=urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:token-exchange`
  - `subject_token=<OIDC ID token>`
  - `subject_token_type=urn:ietf:params:oauth:token-type:id_token`
  - `requested_token_type=urn:chikachow:github-app-installation-access-token`
  - `audience=https://github.com/apps/{github-app-slug}`
  - optional `resource=https://api.github.com/repos/{owner}/{repo}`
  - optional `scope=<github-app-permission-scope>`
- `subject_token` may be either:
  - GitHub Actions OIDC ID token from `https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com`
  - Google service account ID token from `https://accounts.google.com`
- `Authorization` header is rejected in v1 with `401 invalid_client`; the broker does not support OAuth client authentication yet.
- `actor_token`, `actor_token_type`, `client_id`, `client_secret`, `client_assertion`, `client_assertion_type`, and `authorization_details` remain unsupported and rejected.
- Response stays RFC 8693/OAuth-shaped:
  - `access_token`
  - `issued_token_type`
  - `token_type=Bearer`
  - `scope`
  - `expires_in`
  - `Cache-Control: no-store`
  - `Pragma: no-cache`

## Authentication And Policy

- Add issuer-aware subject-token verification:
  - GitHub Actions verifier checks issuer, JWKS, `RS256`, expiry, required claims, exact `audience` match, and `azp` if present.
  - Google service account verifier checks issuer `https://accounts.google.com`, Google JWKS, expiry, `subject_token_type=id_token`, `aud` equals the broker’s configured subject-token audience, `email`, `email_verified=true`, and stable numeric `sub`.
- Use a single issuance policy over one principal and one requested GitHub App token:
  ```ts
  {
    principal:
      | { kind: "github_actions"; repository; ref; workflowRef; eventName; subject; ... }
      | { kind: "google_service_account"; email; subject; audience; ... },
    tokenRequest: { githubAppSlug; resource; permissions; scope }
  }
  ```
- Policy rules are exact-match and deny-by-default:
  ```json
  {
    "id": "gcp-deploy-service-can-write-target-repo",
    "principal": {
      "kind": "google_service_account",
      "email": "deploy-service@PROJECT.iam.gserviceaccount.com",
      "subject": "112010400000000710080"
    },
    "tokenRequest": {
      "githubAppSlug": "example-app",
      "resource": "https://api.github.com/repos/OWNER/REPO",
      "permissions": { "contents": "write" }
    }
  }
  ```
- Keep checked-in policy for v1. Do not move policy to Secret Manager; it is authorization data, not secret material.
- Log structured deny reasons without logging raw OIDC tokens or issued GitHub tokens.

## GCP Platform Integration

- Cloud Run:
  - Deploy one public service: `github-app-token-broker`.
  - Public invocation is intentional; app-level OAuth/OIDC verification is authoritative.
  - Runtime identity: `github-app-token-broker-runtime`.
  - Configure env vars:
    - `BROKER_SUBJECT_TOKEN_AUDIENCE=https://github-app-token-broker.<domain>`
    - `GITHUB_API_BASE_URL=https://api.github.com`
    - GitHub App ID mapping by slug.
- GCP callers:
  - Obtain a Google-signed service account ID token using metadata server, Google auth libraries, IAM Credentials API, Workflows, or equivalent.
  - Set its `aud` to `BROKER_SUBJECT_TOKEN_AUDIENCE`.
  - Send that ID token as `subject_token` in the OAuth token exchange body.
  - Do not rely on Cloud Run’s `Authorization` or `X-Serverless-Authorization` header path for the canonical OAuth exchange.
- Edge and operations:
  - Put Cloud Run behind HTTPS Load Balancer + serverless NEG.
  - Use Cloud Armor for rate limiting and coarse request protection.
  - Keep app-level `64 KiB` body limit.
  - Use Cloud Logging, Monitoring, Error Reporting, and alerting on 5xx, policy-deny spikes, OIDC provider failures, GitHub API failures, and Cloud Armor rate-limit events.
- Secret Manager:
  - Store each GitHub App private key as a separate secret, e.g. `github-app-token-broker-github-app-<slug>-private-key`.
  - Prefer mounted secret volumes for rotation-sensitive keys; Cloud Run fetches mounted secrets from Secret Manager at read time.
  - If using env vars, pin explicit secret versions because Cloud Run resolves secret env vars at instance startup.
  - Grant `roles/secretmanager.secretAccessor` only on required secrets to the runtime service account.
  - Rotate by adding a new secret version, deploying or refreshing the Cloud Run revision, validating issuance, then disabling the old version.
- Delivery:
  - Build with Cloud Build.
  - Store images in Artifact Registry.
  - Deploy with a dedicated deploy service account.
  - Use Workload Identity Federation for CI/CD; no service account keys.

## Implementation Changes

- Rename all deployable service/package/docs/logging identifiers away from `cyspbot`; use `github-app-token-broker`.
- Preserve the existing parser discipline:
  - exactly one non-empty value for required consumed parameters
  - duplicate consumed parameters rejected
  - unsupported extension parameters rejected when non-empty
  - optional empty `scope` and `resource` treated as omitted
- Refactor authentication into:
  - `SubjectTokenVerifier`
  - `GitHubActionsSubjectTokenVerifier`
  - `GoogleServiceAccountSubjectTokenVerifier`
  - `IssuancePrincipal`
- Refactor token policy from GitHub-only principal matching to issuer-aware principal matching.
- Keep token request normalization shared across issuers:
  - GitHub App slug from `audience`
  - canonical GitHub API repo URI from `resource`
  - permissions from OAuth `scope`
- For Google service account subjects, omitted `resource` must be rejected with `invalid_target`; unlike GitHub Actions, there is no source repository claim to default from.
- Keep GitHub Actions default `resource` behavior from the verified GitHub principal repository.

## Test Plan

- OAuth conformance:
  - missing `subject_token` rejected
  - missing `subject_token_type` rejected
  - unsupported `subject_token_type` rejected
  - `Authorization` rejected as unsupported client auth
  - `actor_token` and malformed actor fields rejected
  - success response includes required RFC 8693 fields
- Google service account principal:
  - valid Google ID token in `subject_token` allows matching policy
  - wrong issuer rejected
  - wrong `aud` rejected
  - missing `email`, `email_verified=false`, missing `sub`, expired token, and unknown signing key rejected
  - omitted `resource` rejected for Google principals
- GitHub Actions principal:
  - existing allow/deny tests preserved
  - exact audience match preserved
  - `azp` mismatch rejected
  - omitted `resource` still defaults to source repository
- Policy:
  - GitHub rule cannot match Google principal
  - Google rule cannot match GitHub principal
  - wrong service account email or subject denied
  - wrong GitHub App, resource, or permissions denied with precise reasons
- GCP integration:
  - Cloud Run caller obtains ID token for `BROKER_SUBJECT_TOKEN_AUDIENCE` and sends it as `subject_token`
  - Secret Manager key access works with runtime service account
  - Cloud Armor rate limit triggers before high-volume traffic reaches the app
  - logs contain principal kind, principal identity, policy decision, GitHub App slug, resource, and permissions, but no raw tokens

## Assumptions And Defaults

- Canonical OAuth endpoint is public at the Cloud Run edge and authenticated in application code.
- `subject_token` is the only request principal source.
- Google service account callers use ID tokens, not access tokens.
- `subject_token_type=urn:ietf:params:oauth:token-type:id_token` is preferred for both GitHub Actions and Google service account OIDC tokens.
- No OAuth client authentication in v1.
- Cloud Run IAM-authenticated deployment can be added later as a separate private endpoint, but not mixed into the canonical OAuth-conformant endpoint.
</proposed_plan>
